scoreSummary: COUNCIL ON AMERICAN-ISLAMIC RELATIONS CALIFORNIA shows good impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance (minor risk deductions applied).
zakatClaimEvidence: CAIR-SFBA explicitly states on its website and donation pages, "Zakat-Eligible Giving. Your zakat and sadaqah ensure that our community has somewhere to turn when harm happens close to home." It also provides an option for donors to designate their contribution as Zakat, stating, "My Donation is Intended as Zakat." Furthermore, CAIR-SFBA promotes, "This Ramadan, Give Your Zakat For Justice. By giving your Zakat to CAIR, you ensure that more Muslims facing oppression get the representation they deserve." The organization is also listed as "Zakat eligible" on the Feeling Blessed platform. (Source: https://ca.cair.com/sfba/give-online/)

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Good donor alignment with manageable risk. Evidence quality is solid but still evolving in places.

keyConcerns.headline: CEO compensation is 7.4% of revenue ($1,401,799 on $18,938,214)
keyConcerns.detail: For orgs with revenue $5-50M, CEO pay above 2% of revenue warrants scrutiny.

keyConcerns.headline: Claims 5 geographic areas but no foreign grants on Schedule F
keyConcerns.detail: Organization lists international operations but IRS filings show no foreign grant activity.

components.evidence: Accepts zakat (+2); Muslim-focused organization (+2); Islamic identity (+1); Asnaf: ibn_sabil (+5)
components.improvement_suggestion: Clarify zakat allocation policy and eligible use categories for donors.
components.name: Muslim Donor Fit

alignment.rationale: Alignment 29/50: MEDIUM Muslim donor fit, advocacy cause area

components.evidence: No beneficiary data available for cost-per-beneficiary scoring; good program ratio (≥75%) suggests reasonable delivery
components.improvement_suggestion: Publish beneficiary counts consistently so cost-effectiveness can be assessed.
components.name: Cost Per Beneficiary

components.improvement_suggestion: If reserves are above policy target, document why and publish triggers for planned deployment.
components.name: Financial Health

components.improvement_suggestion: Seek external evaluation or track outcomes over at least 3 years.
components.name: Evidence & Outcomes

components.improvement_suggestion: Document a clear theory of change with an explicit causal pathway.
components.name: Theory of Change

score_details.score_summary: COUNCIL ON AMERICAN-ISLAMIC RELATIONS CALIFORNIA shows good impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance (minor risk deductions applied).

zakat.claim_evidence: CAIR-SFBA explicitly states on its website and donation pages, "Zakat-Eligible Giving. Your zakat and sadaqah ensure that our community has somewhere to turn when harm happens close to home." It also provides an option for donors to designate their contribution as Zakat, stating, "My Donation is Intended as Zakat." Furthermore, CAIR-SFBA promotes, "This Ramadan, Give Your Zakat For Justice. By giving your Zakat to CAIR, you ensure that more Muslims facing oppression get the representation they deserve." The organization is also listed as "Zakat eligible" on the Feeling Blessed platform. (Source: https://ca.cair.com/sfba/give-online/)

all_citations.claim: CAIR-CA reports a program expense ratio of 83.7% and focuses on capacity-building through advocacy.
The organization recorded total revenue of $18,938,214 and holds 14.5 months of working capital with a fundraising efficiency of $0.07 per $1 raised.
The organization is Zakat-eligible and accepts donations to support its civil rights and community programs.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: CAIR-CA demonstrates strong financial health and efficient resource management, though its overall score is moderated by its focus on capacity-building and advocacy which can be harder to quantify than direct service delivery.
areas_for_improvement: Enhancing public impact reporting to provide more granular data on cost per beneficiary.
Expanding third-party transparency certifications to further validate organizational effectiveness.

dimension_explanations.alignment: CAIR-CA offers a medium fit for donors, providing a critical platform for Muslim advocacy and Zakat-eligible giving opportunities [3]. The organization addresses urgent social justice needs, though its alignment score reflects the specialized nature of advocacy-based work compared to direct humanitarian aid [1][3].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization maintains a high program expense ratio of 83.7%, directing $18,938,214 in total revenue toward core missions like civil rights and policy advocacy [1][2]. While its capacity-building model focuses on long-term systemic change, the lack of specific cost-per-beneficiary data results in a medium impact score [1].

baseline_narrative.headline: The Council on American-Islamic Relations California defends the constitutional rights of American Muslims through robust legal advocacy and civil rights protections.
strengths: High program expense ratio of 83.7% ensures the majority of funds directly support civil rights and advocacy initiatives.
Exceptional fundraising efficiency with a cost of only $0.07 per $1 raised.
Strong liquidity position with 14.5 months of working capital available.
baseline_narrative.summary: The Council on American-Islamic Relations California (CAIR-CA) operates as a leading civil rights organization dedicated to protecting the liberties of American Muslims through legal services, policy advocacy, and media relations [1][2]. The organization is recognized as Zakat-eligible, allowing donors to fulfill their religious obligations through contributions that support social justice and community empowerment [3].

all_citations.quote: CAIR works to protect and defend the constitutional rights of American Muslims
Revenue grew from $7,778,802 in 2021 to $17,442,755 in 2023
Grants and other assistance to domestic organizations: $4,158,077

all_citations.claim: Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid
independent board comprising 93% of its 15 members

all_citations.quote: No beneficiary data available for cost-per-beneficiary scoring
CAIR-CA aims to. promote an accurate image of Muslims with our media partners

rich_narrative.amal_score_rationale: CAIR-CA demonstrates strong financial health and efficient resource management, though its overall score is moderated by its focus on capacity-building and advocacy which can be harder to quantify than direct service delivery.

areas_for_improvement.detail: While the organization tracks <cite id="15">5 outcome metrics</cite>, it currently lacks public data for a <cite id="16">cost-per-beneficiary assessment</cite>.
The organization currently has an <cite id="17">absent formal theory of change</cite> document, which could help donors better understand the long-term causal pathway of its advocacy work.

bbb_assessment.summary: The organization maintains high governance standards with a 93% independent board and audited financial statements.

case_against.mitigation_notes: The organization is currently tracking 5 outcome metrics through Candid to improve its evidence base.
risk_factors: <cite id="17">No formal theory of change</cite> documented
case_against.summary: The primary limitation is the <cite id="16">lack of granular beneficiary data</cite>, making it difficult to calculate precise cost-effectiveness compared to direct-service charities.

data_gaps: No formal Theory of Change document
confidence.notes: Strong financial and governance data available; impact data is limited to outcome tracking.

alignment.explanation: The organization is a strong fit for Muslim donors as it <cite id="7">accepts zakat</cite> and serves the <cite id="8">fuqara, masakin, and fisabilillah</cite> categories through civil rights protections.
alignment.improvement: Alignment could be improved by <cite id="20">clarifying the specific zakat allocation policy</cite> and eligible use categories for various program areas.

credibility.explanation: Donors can trust CAIR-CA due to its <cite id="12">Platinum Seal of Transparency</cite> and a large <cite id="13">15-member board with 93% independence</cite>. The organization also undergoes <cite id="19">regular financial audits</cite> to ensure accountability for its $17.4M in revenue.
credibility.improvement: To strengthen credibility further, the organization could <cite id="17">document a clear theory of change</cite> with an explicit causal pathway for its legal and media interventions.

impact.explanation: Impact is driven by a <cite id="4">83.7% program expense ratio</cite> and a delivery model focused on <cite id="18">capacity building and legal defense</cite>. The organization tracks <cite id="15">5 specific outcome metrics</cite> to monitor its progress.
impact.improvement: Impact measurement would benefit from <cite id="16">publishing consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for more rigorous cost-effectiveness analysis.

donor_fit_matrix.evidence_rigor: Grade C - Outcome tracking via Candid

grantmaking_profile.grant_strategy: Distributes funds to local partners to support refugee resettlement and social services.

rich_narrative.headline: The Council on American-Islamic Relations California defends the constitutional rights of American Muslims through robust legal advocacy and civil rights protections.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who prioritize systemic change through legal advocacy and want to fulfill zakat obligations by protecting the civil liberties of the American Muslim community.
donor_motivations: Want to combat Islamophobia through the legal system
Value high financial efficiency and low fundraising overhead
Seek to support a well-established institution with a 30-year track record
Prefer funding systemic advocacy over direct emergency relief
giving_considerations: Impact is measured through long-term policy and legal outcomes rather than immediate individual aid
The organization has a very high liquidity position, suggesting long-term stability
Contributions support a mix of direct legal work and sub-grants to other non-profits
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking high-frequency RCT-level evidence of immediate poverty alleviation or those looking for international humanitarian relief.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade C reflects that while the organization <cite id="15">tracks 5 outcome metrics</cite>, it lacks a formal theory of change and external impact evaluations.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: CAIR-CA creates impact by providing legal counsel to victims of discrimination, engaging with media to correct narratives, and advocating for policy changes that protect Muslim civil rights.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence in advocacy helps donors understand how legal victories and media mentions translate into long-term safety and equity for the community.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: Despite rapid growth, the increasing demand for civil rights defense and legal services in California suggests a significant ongoing funding gap.

organizational_capacity.geographic_reach: California (Los Angeles, San Diego, Sacramento, Orange County)

peer_comparison.differentiator: CAIR-CA's <cite id="4">83.7% program ratio</cite> is significantly higher than the <cite id="9">peer median of 78.7%</cite>.

similar_organizations.differentiator: Similar focus on advocacy and legal services with comparable organizational size.
similar_organizations.name: IMRC

similar_organizations.differentiator: Broader civil liberties focus beyond the Muslim community.
similar_organizations.name: ACLU Foundation

similar_organizations.differentiator: Specific focus on wrongful convictions within the legal advocacy space.
similar_organizations.name: Innocence Project

strengths.detail: The organization directs <cite id="4">83.7% of its total expenses</cite> toward its core programs, significantly exceeding the <cite id="9">peer program ratio median of 78.7%</cite>.
CAIR-CA maintains <cite id="10">14.5 months of working capital</cite> in working capital, providing a strong buffer for long-term advocacy campaigns.
The charity spends only <cite id="11">$0.07 to raise every $1</cite> in contributions, demonstrating highly effective donor acquisition and retention strategies.

strengths_deep_dive: CAIR-CA's financial health is characterized by rapid growth and high transparency. The organization achieved a <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of 49.7%</cite>, growing from approximately $7.7 million in 2021 to over <cite id="2">$17.4 million in 2023</cite>. This growth is supported by a <cite id="12">Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite>, the highest level of recognition for non-profit data sharing. Furthermore, its governance is anchored by an <cite id="13">independent board comprising 93% of its 15 members</cite>, ensuring robust oversight of its <cite id="14">$23.3 million in total assets</cite>.
rich_narrative.summary: The Council on American-Islamic Relations California (CAIR-CA) serves as a primary legal and advocacy shield for the American Muslim community, focusing on <cite id="1">protecting and defending constitutional rights</cite>. Operating across major hubs including Los Angeles, San Diego, and Sacramento, the organization provides critical legal services, media representation, and policy advocacy to combat Islamophobia and civil rights violations. In 2023, CAIR-CA demonstrated significant financial growth, reporting <cite id="2">total revenue of $17,442,755</cite>, which represents a remarkable <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of 49.7%</cite>. This expansion has been managed with high fiscal discipline, maintaining a <cite id="4">program expense ratio of 83.7%</cite>, ensuring that nearly all contributions directly fund its civil rights and advocacy missions. Beyond legal defense, the organization acts as a <cite id="5">significant grantmaker</cite>, distributing over <cite id="6">$4.1 million in domestic grants</cite> to partners like the International Rescue Committee and Jewish Family Services to support broader humanitarian and resettlement efforts. As a <cite id="7">zakat-eligible institution</cite>, CAIR-CA provides a vital avenue for Muslim donors to fulfill religious obligations through social justice work that falls under the categories of <cite id="8">fuqara, masakin, and fisabilillah</cite>.
